diagrams.title: TCP Congestion Control State Machine
diagrams.description: Shows the finite state machine of TCP congestion control phases including Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, and Fast Recovery. Demonstrates how cwnd evolves through these states based on ACK events and timeout/dupACK triggers.
elements: Fast Recovery State (cwnd reduction and retransmission)
diagrams.purpose: Explains the fundamental state transitions that ALL TCP congestion control variants are built upon, providing the foundation for understanding variant-specific behaviors

diagrams.title: Congestion Window Growth Comparison
diagrams.description: Compares the congestion window (cwnd) growth over time for different TCP variants: TCP Tahoe (exponential then linear), TCP Reno (with fast recovery), TCP CUBIC (cubic function), and TCP BBR (model-based). Shows RTT on x-axis and cwnd on y-axis.
elements: TCP Tahoe curve (sawtooth pattern with exponential start)
TCP CUBIC curve (cubic function with inflection point)
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes how different congestion control algorithms adjust their window size, highlighting the key distinguishing feature between loss-based (Tahoe, Reno, CUBIC) and model-based (BBR) approaches

diagrams.title: TCP Variants Classification & Features
diagrams.description: Hierarchical diagram showing the TCP congestion control variant family tree and their key features. Groups variants by generation: Traditional (Tahoe, Reno, NewRENO), High-Speed ( BIC, CUBIC), and Delay-Based (Vegas, BBR).
elements: TCP Tahoe - Slow start, fast retransmit, AIMD
TCP NewRENO - Improved fast recovery, partial ACKs
TCP BIC - Binary search, additive increase
TCP BBR - Bottleneck bandwidth and RTT model
diagrams.purpose: Provides structural overview of variant relationships and key differentiating features, helping students understand the evolution from basic AIMD to modern cubic and model-based approaches
